title: NIC Q-Log File Schema v1
description: Structured log of implementer questions raised during a clean-room conformance experiment. Each entry records whether a spec section was fully determinate (WELL_SPECIFIED_CONFIRMED), required an inference to fill a gap (UNDERSPECIFIED), or contained an internal inconsistency (SPEC_DEFECT). Aggregating these across runs produces a spec-illumination map: which regions are fully determined, which are dark, and whether defects decrease over time.

schema_version.description: Frozen identifier for this schema version. Increment if fields are added or semantics change.

experiment_id.description: Cross-reference to the experiment record (e.g. docs/experiments/nic-cleanroom-001.md).

id.description: Unique identifier within this experiment's log, matching the implementer's own numbering.

section.description: Spec section(s) the question is anchored to (e.g. '§3', '§7.1', '§10, §3').

type.description: WELL_SPECIFIED_CONFIRMED: the spec fully determines behavior; the implementer confirmed this by reading the text — no inference required beyond literal interpretation or vacuous proof. UNDERSPECIFIED: behavior had to be inferred or reconciled; the spec is silent, ambiguous, or only guarantees the well-formed case. SPEC_DEFECT: internal inconsistency in the spec (prose contradicts algorithm, two sections conflict, etc.) — an implementer who trusts one part cannot safely trust another.

subtype.description: Required when type is WELL_SPECIFIED_CONFIRMED; omit for UNDERSPECIFIED and SPEC_DEFECT. EXPLICIT_SPECIFIED: behavior is directly stated as a rule or constraint in the spec text — a future implementer reading the same sentence should reach the same conclusion. These are candidates for behavioral equivalence proofs: 'this implementation computes what the spec text prescribes.' STRUCTURALLY_DETERMINED: behavior is forced by grammar, type-system invariants, or formal structure independent of any spec sentence — the spec rule holds or is unreachable by construction, not because the text says so. These are candidates for invariance-by-construction proofs. IMPORTANT: STRUCTURALLY_DETERMINED entries may be language-conditioned — a rule vacuously satisfied by Rust's type system might require active enforcement in JavaScript. The 'language_conditioned' flag in 'cause' should be set when this applies.

cause.description: Ordered list of causal anchors explaining why this classification holds. Recommended vocabulary (not exhaustive): 'direct_spec_text' (a spec sentence directly resolves this), 'frozen_step_order' (§6's or similar frozen-order framing resolves sequential evaluation questions), 'grammar_impossibility' (the spec's grammar makes the case structurally unreachable), 'type_system_guarantee' (the implementation language's type system enforces the property by construction), 'explicit_exclusion' (spec text explicitly scopes out or marks unconstrained: 'not further constrained,' 'never appears,' etc.), 'logical_consequence' (follows by combining multiple explicit spec rules, no single sentence suffices), 'language_conditioned' (classification would differ for a different implementation language — should flag this when type_system_guarantee is in the list).

summary.description: One- or two-sentence statement of the question exactly as the implementer would have phrased it.

interpretation.description: What the implementer chose to do and why. Omit or leave empty for WELL_SPECIFIED_CONFIRMED entries where no interpretation was needed.

source.description: What the implementer could use to resolve the question. SPEC: spec text alone answered it. CORPUS: required seeing test cases. REFERENCE_IMPL: required seeing a reference implementation. HYBRID: multiple sources needed (typically: implementer used SPEC; orchestrator later cross-checked with REFERENCE_IMPL).

note.description: Any additional detail about how evidence was applied, especially for HYBRID cases.

status.description: CONFIRMED: the implementer's reading was directly validated by the corpus (or is trivially correct per spec). VACUOUSLY_SATISFIED: the spec rule holds trivially due to a language guarantee (no inference, no test case needed). ACCEPTED: for UNDERSPECIFIED entries — implementer made a defensible choice; corpus passed with that choice; no defect in the spec itself. FIXED: for SPEC_DEFECT entries — the spec was corrected. REJECTED: the implementer's choice turned out to be wrong per corpus or reference; requires a follow-up.

spec_patch_applied.description: True if the resolution involved editing the spec document (only applicable to SPEC_DEFECT entries).

corpus_exercises_this.description: True if at least one case in the golden corpus exercises the behavior this question is about. False means this is a 'dark spot' in the corpus that would not be caught by corpus testing alone.

note.description: Any additional resolution detail, including what changed in the spec if spec_patch_applied is true.

classification_rationale.description: Why this entry is classified as its type (not as either of the other two). Should make it possible for a future reviewer to verify the classification independently.

CoverageStats.description: Derived metric over the entries array. Computed at file creation time; re-compute if entries change.
